food_subgroup: Paneer cubes in rich tomato-cashew-butter sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Paneer Butter Masala. Nearest parent is a dairy-based curry. A new concept covering the popular 'makhani' sauce family is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Dairy (paneer) in tomato-butter sauce, North Indian style (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of paneer butter masala is 1 bowl (approximately 200g) — soft paneer cubes in a rich, smooth, orange-red tomato-cashew-cream sauce with a buttery aroma and mild to medium spice.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer Butter Masala — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry F01040002
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in ICMR-NIN under entry F01040002 — Paneer Butter Masala (Paneer Makhani), standard restaurant preparation.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from high-fat, high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate tomato-dairy curry; the cashew fat and cream significantly lower GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 716mg per bowl (200g) — high; restaurant paneer butter masala is typically high in butter, cream, and salt. Calcium from paneer and dairy is beneficial.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit to a half portion and choose reduced-butter preparations. Home cooking allows significant sodium reduction. The calcium benefit does not outweigh the sodium burden at restaurant serving levels.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~28); glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 5. Very low in carbohydrates and very high in fat and protein which prevent blood glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer butter masala is acceptable for diabetic patients in moderate portions — the high fat content (16.5g/100g) does not cause a glycaemic spike. However, the high saturated fat content warrants general moderation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (9.2g/100g) from butter, cream, and paneer may promote inflammation if consumed in excess; tomato lycopene and kasuri methi have modest anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Consume in moderation — limit to 1 bowl twice weekly. The high saturated fat content from butter and cream may exacerbate inflammation in active disease. Reduced-cream preparations are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Butter Masala (also called Paneer Makhani) is one of North India's most celebrated curries — soft fresh paneer cubes simmered in a luxuriously smooth, mildly spiced, orange-red sauce made from puréed tomatoes, fried onion, cashew paste, generous butter, and fresh cream. The sauce is always blended smooth and has a distinctly silky, velvety texture. A signature note of kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves) gives the dish its characteristic aroma. Rich in calcium and vitamin A but high in saturated fat and sodium, making it an indulgence dish best consumed in moderation.
common_user_queries: paneer butter masala vs palak paneer

confidence_reason: Exact match found in ICMR-NIN under entry F01040002 — Paneer Butter Masala, standard preparation.
notes: Contains dairy (paneer, butter, cream) and tree nut (cashew) allergens. Not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 104mg per bowl (200g). Vitamin B12 present from dairy. The trans fat (0.1g/100g) is from dairy natural trans fat (conjugated linoleic acid from butter), not industrial trans fat.
